Transcrição Completa: 45 Modern Show Rate Best Practices - Build Trust Rapidly

[00:15] Obviously, as a group, we've been talking about some overall best practices as is to ensure that you are getting people to show up well framed, answering people's questions prior to calls.
[00:28] And the one thing that I've noticed that we haven't really like just dug deep into on a call like this are the best practices for helping people do their due diligence.

[03:08] Okay? Like for everybody who doesn't know the automation niche, it's those done for you stores that people were selling where they're like done for you Amazon stores, done for you whatever shit store.
[03:22] And at first, like the promise of that niche was you're going to give us tens of thousands of dollars and we're going to give you passive income. [04:20] So to be clear, that's isolated to people having a desire for passive income getting fucked and then now the next things that they ever go to buy, they're going to be so much more aware as a consumer of how things can actually go.

[05:20] Yeah. The answer, just like Haron says, fucking no. Okay. Fucking no. You don't your money gets vaporized and it's gone forever.

[05:42] Okay? Well, now think of that related to this event. So conservatively, tens of thousands of people got burnt on losing tens of thousands of dollars each, some of them more.
[05:55] They didn't get a fucking dollar back in in most instances. and they were offered guarantees and they got fucking burnt. And almost every single one of these businesses declared bankruptcy.

[06:25] And then what they do is is they tell their friends and then their friends tell their friends you know and those are called hand-me-down beliefs.
[06:33] So then what starts to occur is is like the average consumer that is associated to those people and the people who are associated to them and the people who are associated to them and obviously we have the internet.

[07:02] They'll never fucking do a deal again without doing excessive levels of due diligence. They will then think, what will they think about all guarantees from that point onward?

[07:50] Like is is is is a guarantee real anymore? Like do guarantees actually have meaning? Do they have effectiveness? If you used a guarantee, would it actually get the person to convert?
[08:02] As an example, because a lot of people still heavily rely on that where they say, "Hey, if we can't get you this result, like don't worry, we'll give you your money back."
[08:12] Okay, but again, from the perspective of do they actually believe it, right? That's essentially what we want to understand in this entire talk today. Okay? Do they actually believe you?
[08:25] Cuz if the answer is no, we don't want to do it. Okay? That's what this all comes down to when it comes to these like modern due diligence best practices. We are trying to solve around the problem of do they actually believe you?
[08:40] If you are using things that they can consider full of bullshit bullshit practices, then we're doing it wrong.

[13:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Modern Best Practice: Talk About Both Upside AND Downside"
NÚMEROS: 90% success rate, 10% failure rate, 4 months commitment
VISUAL: Comparação visual entre falar apenas do sucesso vs. falar de ambos os resultados possíveis, layout dividido mostrando transparência
[13:05] Okay? Whereas a current best practices is both if it did and if it did not. Okay? Because to be clear, like that's the reality of the situation.
[13:18] Like when we go into a deal as a marketing agency, we straight up tell people as real as it gets in a sales call. We try to tell people as transparently as we possibly can.
[13:30] So, we have people spend an average of $50,000 in ad spend in their first 30 days with us. We cost $15,000. And in some instances, they're also paying out like another 15 to 20 to a sales agency that they're also going to bring on.
[13:48] And we have to be extremely transparent about the risks of that. We have to openly let them know like first of all, we're new to their business. The other people they might be bringing in are new to their business. That alone dramatically increases the probability of failure.
[14:05] What we're going to have that business do is be hyper aware of the risks and we're not going to sugarcoat it at all.
[14:12] Like we just onboarded a new client literally a month ago, okay? They just finished their 30 days and they're happy as shit. Well, let me tell you about how the first two weeks went.
[14:25] A week into the deal, we had a call funnel running. We spent about two grand. I only got four unqualified people through the funnel. And immediately I was like, I got to get this guy to film a whole bunch of new ads.
[14:40] Every single ad that I had to get the guy to refilm took him another like four days. I started off every ad from that point forward with if you have $70,000 in liquid capital or access to 70k in capital, pay attention because I was only getting brokies before.
[15:00] So, we're we're almost 14 days into the deal and I just now get new ads from the guy and we relaunch the call funnel again and now I start getting qualified people.
[15:12] So, the first like two weeks of the deal like the deal went literally nowhere. He's probably shitting himself thinking, "Oh my god, like this is going to fail." Okay.
[15:23] Also, in the second two weeks, I had the guy do a webinar because I told him, I was like, "I got to get something else in motion to be able to dramatically increase the probability we're going to make money in this first 30 days.
[15:38] I'm going to spend 15k out of that 50 on a webinar. I want you to promote it organically, too. Let's get on a call. let's build a little deck together and let's start the process of getting a webinar promoted.
[15:52] We did that. The webinar literally was about 4 days ago and he got about 42 qualified scheduled calls, people with good credit scores, people with 70k uh and thankfully the guy off of the total of about a 65k initial investment already produced a little under 300k in revenue.

[16:20] Okay, that's an example of like a scary deal for the first two weeks though. It's like I can give another example of a client, one of the inner circle members, Jeremy Mosa, referred him to me.
[16:35] There were these two guys, one was named Alex and one was named Gaddam. Alex and Gdam wanted to go and promote this thing they had called like merchant automation.
[16:48] Thankfully, it wasn't an automation business. It was a offer to essentially sell uh transaction terminals like a credit card terminal to a business and then make a percentage of the total sales.
[17:02] And long story short, they were doing a million a month organically through Jeremy doing shoutouts on Instagram and they wanted to introduce paid ads.
[17:12] So we start the process of doing paid ads. Their show rate was 35%. They had a two call close. Their cost per book call for a qualified call was in the high 200s.
[17:25] We're like a month into the deal. They get break even on the initial spend on us and on the ad spend. And they actually pulled ahead profitable by about 10k going into the second 30 days.
[17:40] I told him, I was like, "We're going to have to spend a lot more money with these current statistics while we improve things in order to get to the point where we're actually making some money." Do you guys agree to that? They agreed.
[17:55] We get six days into the deal for the second 30 days, and they tell me they want to pull back spend while we improve the show rate.
[18:05] I respond to that message instantly, firing them by asking for their wire information. I kicked them back the 30K for the 15 for the first month and 15 for the second month that they had paid, and we walked from the deal.
[18:20] It's like to be clear, when I talk about that stuff in a sales call, I don't just want to talk about the people that we've taken on as a client and made $2.3 million for in the first 30 days.
[18:33] I don't just want to talk about the deals where we've done a million dollars a month within 60 days or 90 days because that skews the perception dramatically that that's the only like they don't believe me that that's the only thing that could happen because it's not the only thing that could happen.
[18:50] There's downside to every decision, okay? And they need to know what the downside is in a transparent way and they need to hear it from you. Otherwise, they go and look it up themselves.
[19:03] Okay? I was talking to Cameron who sells a Forex trading service. And it's not just Forex to be fair to him. He does a trading bot that also also can be paired with pretty much anything like an index, an indicy, just traditional stock, commodities, and forex.
[19:22] Okay? And it's not like one of those stupid fucking bots that everybody was selling where people would just put money into the bot and like never see it again and it just gets vaporized, you know, even though they claim it's making money.
[19:35] No, in reality, like Cameron's bot, you actually own the bot. He doesn't even get a rev share from it. You own the bot. You just pay an upfront fee and you own the bot. You can customize it however you want. He has a whole bunch of scripts. Cameron actually does really fucking good.
[19:52] Here's the thing though, Cameron's industry. Okay, what do you guys think about Forex bots? When I say that right there, just again put a good or bad in the chat. What do you think about Forex bots?

[36:52] Okay, dollars lost dramatic amounts of value after the 70s. [58:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Ryan Story - Radical Transparency Example"
NÚMEROS: 500+ customers, 1 dispute, $5,000 program cost
VISUAL: Narrativa completa de um caso de disputa, mostrando transparência extrema e como lidar com exceções
[58:38] Ryan was a younger guy. He was moving out of his house. Okay? And Ryan, due to the fact he was moving out of his house, needed some money. And Ryan decided to dispute the transaction with us for Master Internet Marketing.
[58:55] And we were super confused by it. Ryan was extremely active in the community chat. I even looked up Ryan inside of our course and I saw that he consumed without exaggeration literally the entirety of the course.
[59:12] The hundreds of videos that come along with it, every single live class that's 3 to 5 hours long. And again, he was extremely active in the community. It was shocking when Ryan did this.
[59:28] First thing I did was just try to call Ryan a few times throughout the day to try to understand what occurred and what we did wrong. I couldn't get a hold of them.
[59:40] Generally, when somebody does something like that, it means that they did something wrong, not us. And I wanted to understand why. Like, what happened? Maybe if we did do something wrong, I wanted to be able to fix it, rectify it.
[59:55] Well, I couldn't get a hold of Ryan. And look, Ryan hired me as a mentor. I care about Ryan. So, what I did is I looked on white pages.com, which is just a public directory of information, and I looked up Ryan's information.
[1:00:12] And I can see relatives of Ryan's. I saw his father. I called his father's cell phone, and I get a hold of Ryan's dad, and I explained to his dad. I'm like, "Listen, I'm really concerned. Like, this guy hired me to be his mentor and he disputed a transaction with us today.
[1:00:32] And he consumed literally the entirety of the material. He was really active in our community. It seemed like he was loving it. Out of nowhere, he disputed it. I I just can't get a hold of the guy. I'm trying to understand what's going on. Is he okay? Like, did something happen?"
[1:00:50] And Ryan's dad explains to me that he was moving out. He was moving out from Tampa and he was moving on his own. And uh Ryan's dad calls Ryan. Ryan answers and he three-ways the call with me.
[1:01:08] So now I finally get a hold of Ryan and I'm like, "Ryan, what happened?" And Ryan just almost breaks down on the call, man. You know how when somebody's about to cry, they kind of sound a little choked up and their voice gets a little shaky?
[1:01:25] This is like a fucking 20-year-old kid, man. Probably dealing with something that brought him some shame. And he starts to explain that he just didn't have enough money and he needed money and he was one of the most recent transactions that he could think of calling.
[1:01:45] And he did it with many others, not just us. He did it with many others, too. He called his bank and he acted like his card had gotten compromised and he charged back as many transactions as they would allow him to to get the money that he needed to move out.
[1:02:05] Well, Ryan's dad ended up making it right, but he wired us money. So, if you look in my payment processor here, you'll see Ryan as a dispute. Ryan wasn't unhappy. Ryan needed money. And unfortunately, he used the Bank of Jeremy for that.
[1:02:25] But you'll notice out of the other 520 transactions, there's not a single person who's disputed otherwise. There's not even a single person we've had to refund.
[1:02:38] Okay, that's an example of fucking due diligence. You see what I mean? That's an example of like a modern practice that actually works that people want to know.
[1:02:50] Because do you know how many other fucking different businesses are out there that they've been absolutely hosed by? And again, like I said, sophistication is no longer niche specific. Okay?
[1:03:05] Sophistication has bled out to all of their other consumer behavior at this point. Okay, look at Joe's business. He's dealing with fucking 60 to 80 year olds. That's 60 to 80 years of getting fucked by businesses.
[1:03:22] You don't think that they're going to do research on Joe and just read one? All it takes is that one line. I bought this. It was a ripoff. How fast do you think that person's gonna abandon the buying process just from reading that single line?
[1:03:40] Well, apparently 50 to 55% of the time that's all it takes for them to fucking bail. But if Joe had a proper framing video, as you all need nowadays, okay, that legitimately discloses the risks of doing business with you, okay?

[1:41:42] So you can see in the USA like men of action price Reddit has no searches but Michael Sartine 260 um Michael Sartine 2900 and you can see which uh you can use Seamrush or you can scour the internet manually and get a list of all the Reddit threads that are appearing and then where they rank according to the platforms.
[1:42:08] You can use I search from or Seamrush. So we've been doing the same analysis for a few people in the inner circle as well and we're having conversations with them.
[1:42:22] Obviously, you can hire an SEO agency to remove specific Reddit threads where you attack those specific Reddit threads. You can get them off the first page of Google in literally 3 to 6 months.
[1:42:40] And then the other thing I would really encourage you do, which we're doing with a few other inner circle members, is just like start building positive brand SER. Okay?
[1:42:52] So, what that means is you don't have to go on entrepreneur.com and get a backlink. That would cost you thousands. But you can get these back links or guest posts off reputable sources where you have articles that occupy the first page of Google even before Reddit threads start to appear.
[1:43:15] And you can make sure that your own website or other mini websites rank on the first page of Google by just doing SEO on them. So that when people type in your name and Reddit and your name and scam, you've already have some positive brand SER before the Reddit threads or the negative ones start appearing.
[1:43:38] And that will make it a lot more difficult for those Reddit threads to influence the the discourse about you when they suddenly appear, you know, unbeknownst to you. Makes sense.

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$100 milhões perdidos no nicho de automation stores (2019-2023)
5,132 clientes atendidos (caso Joe)
91% taxa de sucesso
9% taxa de falha
0.7% taxa de disputa no Stripe (99.3% satisfação)
547 reviews no Trust Pilot (4.8/5 estrelas)
45-50% show rate atual (Joe)
60-70% show rate antes de Reddit thread (Joe)
85% show rate (Devon - exemplo positivo)
15% show rate em webinar (cliente Airbnb arbitrage)
$5,000 preço do programa (Joe)
$100/semana custo mencionado em reviews
$700 USD/mês mínimo mencionado
$50,000 gasto médio em ads (primeiro mês)
$15,000 custo da agência
$65,000 investimento total → $300,000 revenue (caso de sucesso)
$24,000 oferta (Jordan & Jacob)
500+ clientes Master Internet Marketing
1 disputa em 500+ transações
60-80 anos idade média clientes (Joe)
4 Reddit threads encontradas (cliente Airbnb)
3-4 threads recomendadas por ano
3-6 meses para remover threads do Google (SEO)
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Confirmation Page Video Moderno:
Ajudar ativamente na due diligence
Falar sobre UPSIDE e DOWNSIDE
Endereçar objeções proativamente
Mostrar provas não-manipuláveis (Stripe dashboard, etc.)
Screen share de reviews negativas e positivas
Framing de críticas antes que cliente as encontre
Reddit Strategy:
Reddit = plataforma #1 de due diligence atual
Criar 3-4 threads ao longo do ano
Distribuir clientes por trimestre em threads diferentes
Pedir feedback genuíno (não forçar)
Monitorar threads existentes
Engajar clientes satisfeitos para responder
Framing de Objeções:
Preço: contextualizar inflação, valor do dólar ao longo do tempo
Garantias: explicar que não protegem contra falência
Reviews negativas: mostrar que maioria não comprou
Dispute rate como métrica de satisfação
Comparar com produtos do dia-a-dia (Purell 99.99%)
Trust Building Assets:
Vídeos breakout para FAQ (1-5 minutos cada)
Seção massiva de testimonials
Screenshots de resultados
Transparência sobre falhas e como são tratadas
Histórias detalhadas (exemplo Ryan)
SEO Defense:
Google Keyword Planner / Semrush para monitorar
Criar brand SERP positivo antes de threads negativas
Guest posts em sites reputáveis
Vídeos YouTube endereçando "scam" keywords
Remover threads negativas (3-6 meses via SEO agency)
ESTRUTURA:
Confirmation pages com múltiplas camadas
Vídeo principal de due diligence (transparente)
Breakout videos para perguntas específicas
FAQ visual com timestamps
Testimonials de clientes conhecidos e desconhecidos
Email sequence de hammer strategy
Follow-up estruturado
PROBLEMAS IDENTIFICADOS:
Sophistication do consumidor aumentou (cross-industry)
Garantias perderam credibilidade (falências)
Trust Pilot visto como manipulável
Vídeos genéricos de confirmação são inúteis
Reddit threads não endereçadas matam show rate
Boomers têm percepção distorcida de valor ($)
AI deepfakes aumentando desconfiança geral
Reviews de 1 estrela têm peso desproporcional
Contexto escondido em UI do Reddit (+4 replies button)
Google snippets podem ser enganosos
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Automation niche: ~$100M perdidos (2019-2023)
Caso sucesso recente: $65K invest → $300K revenue (30 dias)
Merchant automation: $1M/mês orgânico antes de ads
Show rate 70% → 45% = perda significativa de revenue
Cost per qualified call: high $200s
Master Internet Marketing: $5,000/pessoa x 500+ = $2.5M+
DURAÇÃO: 1h 49min 52seg FORMATO: Group coaching call / Masterclass ao vivo
